subject: ap-government
title: Argument Essay — Social Media and Participatory Democracy
stimulus: Social media has changed the way many Americans communicate about politics. Develop an argument as to whether the use of social media has helped or hindered participatory democracy.

parts.prompt: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning. (Use at least one piece of evidence from one of the following foundational documents: First Amendment to the Constitution of the United States, Federalist No. 10, "Letter from a Birmingham Jail".)
rubric_criteria: 1 pt (Row A — Thesis/Claim): Responds to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis establishing a line of reasoning about whether social media has helped or hindered participatory democracy.

Examples that earn the point: 'Using social media helps participatory democracy because users can improve their political knowledge.' OR 'By connecting people to multiple institutions and political processes, social media has helped participatory democracy in the United States.' OR 'Social media has hindered participatory democracy by making Americans less certain about political information.' OR 'When social media is used to spread negative information about politics, it makes citizens less willing to participate in the democratic process.'
parts.scoring_notes: Must take a clear position. Generic statements about social media changing communication do not earn the point.

parts.prompt: Support your claim with at least TWO pieces of specific and relevant evidence. One piece of evidence must come from one of the foundational documents listed. A second piece of evidence can come from any other foundational document or from course concepts.
rubric_criteria: 0 pts: No accurate evidence, or evidence not relevant to the topic.
1 pt (Row B — 1 point): One piece of evidence relevant to the topic.
2 pts (Row B — 2 points): One piece of specific and relevant evidence supports the claim OR two pieces relevant to the topic (the latter does not require Row A).
3 pts (Row B — 3 points): Two pieces of specific and relevant evidence, one from a listed document (First Amendment, Federalist 10, or Letter from Birmingham Jail) (requires Row A).

Examples of acceptable evidence: 'The First Amendment guarantees that people have the right to communicate their needs to their elected representatives.' 'The First Amendment makes it easier for social media to distribute false information.' 'Federalist No. 10 argues that factions are a threat to democracy.' 'Letter from a Birmingham Jail urged citizens to take direct action to seek equality and justice.' 'Social media can be used to learn more about candidates and the election process.' 'Presidents have used social media to encourage voters to support their policies.' 'Misinformation on social media has led to violent reactions from citizens.'
parts.scoring_notes: To earn 3 points, must have earned Row A, and one piece of evidence must come from the First Amendment, Federalist 10, or Letter from Birmingham Jail.

parts.prompt: Use reasoning to explain why your evidence supports your claim or thesis.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt (Row C — Reasoning): Uses reasoning (classification, process, causation, or comparison) to explain how evidence supports an argument relevant to the prompt.

Examples that earn the point: 'Social media gives us better access to what's happening in politics, and when we know what's going on, we can act to make things better — that's good for participatory democracy.' 'Social media makes it easier for our representatives to respond to citizens' needs, which improves efficacy and increases participation further.' 'If the First Amendment allows citizens to spread misinformation, the quality of political knowledge will continue to decrease, and people will participate less in politics.' 'When social media intensifies political polarization, people can be discouraged from participating in the political process and may tune out politics altogether.'
parts.scoring_notes: Must have provided at least one piece of specific and relevant evidence. Must explain the connection between evidence and the argument about participatory democracy.

parts.prompt: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using rebuttal or refutation.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt (Row D — Alternate Perspective): Responds to an opposing perspective using rebuttal or refutation. Must describe an alternate perspective AND rebut or refute it.

Examples that earn the point: 'Even if social media has introduced people to questionable information, it has encouraged them to become involved in politics anyway, which is good for participatory democracy.' 'Even though the rise of ideologically driven news sources has created some confusion, they are also giving elected representatives a better perspective on what the people want, which is good for participatory democracy.' 'While some may argue that social media improves democracy because it improves linkage institutions, it has also harmed the credibility of news sources, which is ultimately worse for participatory democracy.' 'Many would argue that social media gives more people an opportunity to express their opinions to a larger audience, but that could lead to an overwhelming amount of information that can discourage people from wanting to engage in the political process.'
parts.scoring_notes: Must have earned Row A. Must describe an alternate perspective AND rebut or refute it. Merely acknowledging an alternate perspective without engaging with it does not earn the point.
